title: Chapter 2: American Red-necked Grebe. "Holboell's Grebe."
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: A common winter resident along the coast of Vancouver Island and Mainland. Winter resident on Okanagan Lake. (_Brooks_.) Not common at Metlakatla. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.) Breeds on many of the lakes along the Cariboo Road above Clinton. (_Fannin_.)

title: Chapter 3: Horned Grebe.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: A common winter resident on the coast, and very common in the straits near Victoria, in April. Brooks reports it at Okanagan Lake all winter. Breeds on the lakes from Okanagan to Dense Lake. (_Fannin_.) Rare at Metlakatla. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.)

title: Chapter 5: Pied-billed Grebe.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common resident on Vancouver Island; it breeds on lakes close to Victoria. Common resident on Island and Mainland; breeds throughout its range. (_Fannin_.)

title: Chapter 9: Red-throated Loon.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: I found this species quite common on Barkley Sound, V. I., April 19th, 1894, and fairly common near Esquimalt. Mr. Brooks reports it at Chilliwhack.
Family ALCIDÆ. Auks, Murres and Puffins.

title: Chapter 13: Cassin's Auklet.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: The entire coast line of the Province and West Coast of Vancouver Island. (_Fannin_.) This species was seen in the Gulf of Georgia, between Salt Spring Island and Nanaimo, May 7th, 1887. (_Macoun_.)

title: Chapter 14: Ancient Murrelet.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not common. West Coast of Vancouver Island and taken in the Straits, near Victoria. Rev. J. H. Keen reports it rare on Queen Charlotte Islands.

title: Chapter 15: Marbled Murrelet.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: An abundant resident along the coast of British Columbia; have taken it at Skidegate, Queen Charlotte Islands, in August, 1895. Rare at Metlakatla. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.) Breeds on Vancouver Island, and on some of the smaller islands in the Gulf of Georgia, and along many of the inlets of the Mainland. (_Fannin_.)

title: Chapter 17: California Murre, Guillemot.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: A resident along the coast of Vancouver Island and Mainland. Breeds on West Coast of Vancouver Island.

title: Chapter 21: Ivory Gull. Snow Gull.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: One specimen shot at Dease Lake, Cassiar, by James Porter, September, 1889. Another specimen was shot at Okanagan Lake in November, 1897, by Mr. J. T. Studley, and presented to the Museum. Mr. A. C. Brooks also reports it from Okanagan.

title: Chapter 25: American Herring Gull.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: An abundant resident on the coast. It breeds on the coast and in the interior of Mainland. Common at Metlakatla. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.) Chilliwhack and Okanagan. (_Brooks_.)

title: Chapter 27: Ring-billed Gull.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common along the coast of Vancouver Island and Mainland. Common in winter at Okanagan Lake and Chilliwhack. (_Brooks_.) Breeds in the Interior, especially to the northward. (_Fannin_.)

title: Chapter 28: Short-billed Gull.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common winter resident along the coast of Vancouver Island and Mainland. Common in Lower Fraser Valley. (_Brooks_.) During the early part of May, 1891, I saw quite a number on the lakes of the Cariboo District, where it probably breeds. (_Fannin_.)

title: Chapter 30: Bonaparte's Gull.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: An abundant resident throughout the Province, and found in great numbers about the inlets and rivers in March and April; it breeds in the interior of the Province. I have also seen it on the coast in June and July.

title: Chapter 33: Arctic Tern.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: From Dease Lake south, through the interior of the Mainland to Okanagan Lake. Two specimens--one taken at Dease Lake by James Porter, and another shot at Okanagan Lake by A. C. Brooks, September, 1807.

title: Chapter 34: Black Tern.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not common. I observed it at Okanagan, June, 1895. Taken at Sumas and Okanagan Lake by A. C. Brooks, Burrard Inlet, Eraser River and interior of Mainland. (_Fannin_.)

title: Chapter 36: Short-tailed Albatross.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: In April, 1894, I found this species quite common in the Pacific Ocean, near Cape Beale.
Tolerably common on both coasts of Vancouver Island, but more abundant on the West Coast; a few have been taken in the straits off Victoria Harbour. (_Fannin_.)

title: Chapter 39: Dark-bodied Shearwater.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: During the fall of 1895 Dr. C. F. Newcombe found this species common on the West Coast of Queen Charlotte Islands. Since then one was picked up dead on the beach near Victoria.

title: Chapter 41: Gray Fork-tailed Petrel.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Found along both coasts of Vancouver Island, but more common on the Pacific Coast; a few have been taken in the Straits near Victoria. Very rare at Metlakatla; one picked up exhausted. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.)

title: Chapter 42: Leach's Fork-tailed Petrel. White-rumped Petrel.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: West Coast of Vancouver Island. Four specimens taken in Hecate Strait, July, 1904. One specimen found dead on beach off Beacon Hill, Victoria, November, 1893.

title: Chapter 44: Brandt's Cormorant. Pencilled Cormorant.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Two specimens were killed in the Straits, near Victoria, by D. E. Campbell, April 19th, 1897. Later in the month another specimen was found on the beach at Beacon Hill.

title: Chapter 45: Violet-green Cormorant.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: This is the most abundant Cormorant in the Province, and is found along both coasts of the Island, and has been taken as far north as Port Simpson. Breeds on islands close to Sidney Island. Common at Metlakatla. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.)

title: Chapter 46: American White Pelican.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not common. Four specimens taken--Sicamous, October, 1890; Alexandria Bridge, September, 1895; Tranquille, August, 1897; Comox, September 8th, 1900. Mr. Brooks reports it from Chilliwhack and Okanagan. Said to breed in the Chilcotin country. (_Fannin_.)

title: Chapter 47: California Brown Pelican.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not common. One specimen in the Museum killed at Race Rocks, January, 1897. Seen at Sumas Lake. (_Brooks_.)
Family ANATIDÆ. Mergansers, Ducks, Geese and Swans.

title: Chapter 48: American Merganser.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Found throughout the Province, have taken it on West Coast of Vancouver Island in May; also on Queen Charlotte Islands in July, 1895, I took four young birds. Brooks reports it found on Okanagan Lake all winter.

title: Chapter 49: Red-breasted Merganser.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Found distributed throughout the Province. I saw them at Sumas Lake May 17th, 1904. Rev. J. H. Keen says common at Metlakahtla, Chilliwhack and Okanagan. (_Brooks_.)

title: Chapter 55: Green-winged Teal.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: An abundant resident, Breeds in the interior of the Mainland. Common on the coast throughout the winter. Rare on Queen Charlotte Islands. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.)

title: Chapter 58: Shoveller. Spoon-bill.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: A common resident on the Mainland, rarely met with on Vancouver Island. Brooks says common resident in Lower Fraser Valley about Sumas Lake, has also taken it on Okanagan.

title: Chapter 62: Canvas-back Duck.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: A winter resident on the coast. Brooks has taken it on Okanagan Lake. I found them in pairs on the lakes near 108-Mile House, Cariboo Road, in April and May, 1891, where I was told they breed. (_Fannin_).

title: Chapter 67: Barrow's Golden-eye.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not common. It winters on the coast; a few have been taken on Vancouver Island. Brooks has taken it at Okanagan. Fannin found it plentiful on the lakes along the Cariboo Road, May, 1891.

title: Chapter 70: Harlequin Duck.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: A common resident on the coast. I found it breeding at Bear River, Vancouver Island, May, 1894. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack. Rare at Queen Charlotte Islands. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.)

title: Chapter 71: American Black Scoter.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not common; has been taken at Victoria and Port Simpson. Fannin saw a small flock on a lake near 108-Mile House, Cariboo Road, 10th May, 1891. Fairly common at Matlakatla. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.)

title: Chapter 73: Surf Scoter. Sea Coot.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Abundant resident along the coasts of Vancouver Island and Mainland. Found at Chilliwhack and Okanagan. (_Brooks_.) Common at Metlakatla. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.)

title: Chapter 74: Ruddy Duck.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not common on the Coast; a few are taken in the winter. Fannin found it breeding in the lakes along the Cariboo Road, May, 1891. Brooks has seen it at Chilliwhack and Okanagan.

title: Chapter 77: American White-fronted Goose.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Abundant on the Coast in winter. Breeds both on Island and Mainland. The young have been taken on Cowichan Lake. (_Fannin_.) Brooks has taken it at Sumas and Okanagan Lakes. Common at Metlakatla. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.)

title: Chapter 86: Trumpeter Swan.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: I have only seen this bird at Deaso Lake, Cassiar. (_Fannin_.) Mr. Brooks has found it at Sumas and Okanagan.

title: Chapter 87: White-faced Glossy Ibis.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Only two specimens known to be taken in the Province, one on Salt Spring Island, the other at mouth of Fraser River. Check List of B. C. Birds, 1891. (_Fannin_.)

title: Chapter 90: Northwest Coast Heron.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: In August, 1895, I found this form, now named after the late John Fannin by Frank M. Chapman, quite common at Skidegate, Queen Charlotte Islands.

title: Chapter 91: Snowy Heron.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Rare in British Columbia. Two specimens collected at Burrard Inlet, May, 1879. (_Fannin_.) One specimen is in the Museum.

title: Chapter 93: Sandhill Crane.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common throughout the Province; it breeds in the interior of Mainland; numbers pass over Victoria in the spring and autumn migrations.

title: Chapter 98: Northern Phalarope.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Abundant along the coast of Island and Mainland in the spring and autumn. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack and Okanagan. Fannin has taken it at Burrard Inlet in July.

title: Chapter 101: Long-billed Dowitcher. Red-breasted Snipe.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Tolerably abundant throughout the Province. I found it quite common at Clayoquot, West Coast Vancouver Island, in May, 1894. Fannin says breeds in the interior. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack. Fairly common at Metlakatla. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.)

title: Chapter 106: BAIRD'S SANDPIPER.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Distributed along the coast of Island and Mainland, Rev. J. H. Keen reports it rare at Metlakatla. Taken at Chilliwhack and Okanagan (_Brooks_); and at Clover Point, Victoria (_E. M. Anderson_).

title: Chapter 107: LEAST SANDPIPER.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common along the coast of Island and Mainland. I found it common at Clayoquot in May, 1894; I have also taken it near Victoria in July. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack and Okanagan. Fannin has taken it at 108-Mile House, Cariboo Road, in May.

title: Chapter 110: WESTERN SEMIPALMATED SANDPIPER.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Abundant in the fall along the Coast. I also found it very common at Clayoquot, May, 1894, and have taken it near Victoria in July. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack.

title: Chapter 113: GREATER YELLOW-LEGS.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common along the coast in winter; have taken it at Clayoquot in May. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack and Okanagan. I found it breeding around the lakes at Clinton, May, 1891. (_Fannin_.)

title: Chapter 118: WANDERING TATLER.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Tolerably common along the coast of Island and Mainland. I took it at Clayoquot, May, 1894, and at Skidegate, Queen Charlotte Islands, August 5th, 1895.

title: Chapter 119: BARTRAMIAN SANDPIPER.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: One specimen shot at Comox, August 20th, 1895, by W. B. Anderson. Another was taken at l50-Mile House, Cariboo Road, May 16th, 1901, by A. C. Brooks.

title: Chapter 121: SPOTTED SANDPIPER.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: This bird is found along the entire coast; I have taken it at Clayoquot Vancouver Island, and at Skidegate, Queen Charlotte Islands, July 26th, 1895. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack and Okanagan.

title: Chapter 123: HUDSONIAN CURLEW.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not common, but distributed along the coast of Island and Mainland. I have taken it at Clayoquot in May, 1894. W. B. Andersen has taken it at Port Simpson, and J. Maynard at Cadboro Bay, near Victoria.

title: Chapter 125: AMERICAN GOLDEN PLOVER.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common on the coast during migrations. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack and Okanagan. Rev. J. H. Keen reports it common at Metlakatla. W. B. Anderson has taken it at Port Simpson.

title: Chapter 127: SEMIPALMATED PLOVER.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not common. I took two specimens on the West Coast, Vancouver Island--one at Ucluelet, April 28th, 1894, the other at Clayoquot, May, 1894. A. H. Maynard shot one at Cadboro Bay, Victoria.

title: Chapter 128: SURF-BIRD.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not uncommon along the entire coast line of the Province. I found it quite common at Clayoquot, May, 1894. It has been taken at Port Simpson by W. B. Anderson.

title: Chapter 130: BLACK TURNSTONE.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common along the entire coast of the Province. I have taken it at Clayquot in May, 1894, and I found it very common at Skidegate, Queen Charlotte Islands, in July, 1895, and it is found near Victoria throughout the winter.

title: Chapter 131: BLACK OYSTER-CATCHER.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Abundant along the entire coast of Island and Mainland. Breeds throughout its range. Common on Queen Charlotte Islands, at Skidegate.

title: Chapter 143: COLUMBIAN SHARP-TAILED GROUSE. PRAIRIE CHICKEN.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: An abundant resident east of Cascade Range through the southern portions of the Province. E. P. Venables reports it common near Vernon.

title: Chapter 144: SAGE GROUSE.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Three specimens taken by Mr. G. B. Martin at Osoyoos Lake in October, 1864. (Check List of B. C. Birds, 1891. _Fannin_.)

title: Chapter 145: RING-NECKED PHEASANT.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Abundant on Vancouver Island and in the Lower Fraser River Valley and other portions of the Mainland. Introduced from China; now thoroughly naturalised.

title: Chapter 147: PASSENGER PIGEON.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Mentioned in John Keast Lord's "Naturalist in British Columbia" (1866). If it ever did occur here, it is now, without doubt, extinct. (_Fannin_.)

title: Chapter 148: MOURNING DOVE.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not common on Vancouver Island. I found it quite common in the Okanagan in June, 1895. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack. Rev. J. H. Keen reports it rare at Metlakatla.

title: Chapter 149: CALIFORNIA VULTURE.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: In September, 1880, I saw two of these birds at Burrard Inlet. It is more than probable they are accidental visitants here. Lord says: Mouth of Fraser River; seldom visits the Interior. (_Fannin_.)

title: Chapter 150: TURKEY VULTURE.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Tolerably common on Vancouver Island and portions of the Mainland. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack and Okanagan.
Family FALCONIDÆ. Falcons, Hawks, Eagles, etc.

title: Chapter 151: MARSH HAWK.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common on Vancouver Island and Mainland. Abundant on the Mainland. Partially migratory; a few are found throughout the winter at Ladners. (_Fannin_.) Chilliwhack and Okanagan. (_Brooks_.)

title: Chapter 156: WESTERN RED-TAILED HAWK.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: An abundant resident on Vancouver Island and coast of Mainland. Taken at Chilliwhack and Okanagan. (_Brooks_.) Rare at Massett, Queen Charlotte Islands. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.)

title: Chapter 161: BALD EAGLE.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: An abundant resident throughout the entire Province, on Island and Mainland. Breeds throughout its range. Common on Queen Charlotte Islands.

title: Chapter 165: PEALE'S FALCON.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Found chiefly along the coasts of Mainland and Vancouver Island. Several specimens have been taken at Cadbora Bay, near Victoria. Mr. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack. Rare at Metlakatla. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.)

title: Chapter 170: AMERICAN OSPREY. FISH HAWK.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common throughout the Province, on Mainland and Island, including Queen Charlotte Islands. Their nest is a huge heap of sticks, usually on the broken top of a tree.

title: Chapter 173: NORTHERN SPOTTED OWL.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Rare resident at Chilliwhack and Mount Lehman (_Brooks_.) The late John Fannin had this bird on his first list of B. C. birds, he having taken it at Burrard Inlet some years ago, but in his lists of 1891 and 1898 he dropped it.

title: Chapter 176: SAW-WHET OWL.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not common on Vancouver Island; several have been taken near this city. Tolerably at Chilliwhack and common in winter at Okanagan. (_Brooks_.)

title: Chapter 183: SNOWY OWL.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: A resident in the northern portions of the Province, south in winter to mouth of Fraser River, Chilliwhack and Okanagan, occasionally to Vancouver Island. Rev. J. H. Keen reports it rare at Metlakahtla.

title: Chapter 184: American Hawk Owl.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not common, but found throughout the southern part of the Province from the Coast to Okanagan. Rare on Vancouver Island. It has been taken at Victoria, Sardis, Grand Prairie and Vernon.

title: Chapter 185: Burrowing Owl.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Not common, but is found in suitable localities in the southern portions of the Province from Chilliwhack to Okanagan. Mr. Brooks has taken three specimens at Chilliwhack. Rare at Vernon. (_E. P. Venables_.)

title: Chapter 187: California Pygmy Owl.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common throughout the southern portions of the Province, including Vancouver Island.

title: Chapter 188: Western Yellow-billed Cuckoo. California Cuckoo.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: A regular summer resident in the southwestern portions of the Province. It breeds on Vancouver Island near Victoria. Mr. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack.

title: Chapter 190: Northern Hairy Woodpecker.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common resident on the Mainland east of Cascade Mountains. I found it quite common at Sicamous, May, 1895. Mr. Brooks reports having taken it several times at Chilliwhack.

title: Chapter 195: Batchelder's Woodpecker.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common throughout the interior of the Province, along the C. P. R., at Ducks, and at Vernon. Mr. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack and Okanagan.

title: Chapter 203: Northern Pileated Woodpecker.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common resident throughout the Province, on Vancouver Island and Mainland through the interior to Okanagan. Mr. Brooks found it scarce in the Cariboo District.

title: Chapter 204: Lewis's Woodpecker.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Abundant through the interior of the Province, at Vernon and Grand Prairie. I found it quite common at Chilliwhack this year. A summer resident on Vancouver Island.

title: Chapter 207: North-western Flicker.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Abundant resident west of Cascade Mountains; very common on Vancouver Island.

title: Chapter 211: Black Swift.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common near Victoria during migration. Rev. J. H. Keen says only pass over at Metlakatla. Said to breed in mountains back of Chilliwhack and in the neighbourhood of Comox. (_Fannin_.) Taken at Chilliwhack and Okanagan. (_Brooks_.)

title: Chapter 228: Streaked Horned Lark.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Spring and autumn migrant on Vancouver Island. Have taken it near Victoria in September. Mr. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack. West of Cascades; taken at Burrard Inlet and Vancouver Island (_Fannin_). Fort Simpson by W. B. Anderson.

title: Chapter 229: Dusky Horned Lark.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Chiefly east of Cascades. I have never known it to be taken on Vancouver Island. Mr. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack and Okanagan. Chas. de B. Green has taken it at Osoyoos.

title: Chapter 237: Northern Raven.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: A resident throughout the Province; more common on the Coast. I have seen it quite common at Clayoquot, V. I., and in August, 1895, it was very common at Skidegate, Queen Charlotte Islands. Mr. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack and Okanagan. Common at Metlakatla. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.)

title: Chapter 241: Blue Crow. Piñon Jay.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Rocky Mountain region to the Pacific Coast Ranges, but rather more southerly; north to British Columbia; south to Lower California.--"Key to N. A. Birds (Coues), Fifth Edition."

title: Chapter 243: Cowbird.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: A summer resident. I have found it fairly common in the neighbourhood of Ducks, on C. P. R., and Okanagan. It is a rare straggler on Vancouver Island; it has been taken near Victoria. Mr. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack. Rev. J. H. Keen reports it rare at Metlakatla.

title: Chapter 244: Yellow-headed Blackbird.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: A rare summer resident on the Mainland. I have heard of it at Vernon. Fannin has found it above Clinton, on the Cariboo Road. Brooks has taken it at Chilliwhack.

title: Chapter 248: Bullock's Oriole.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: A fairly common summer resident, chiefly east of Cascades. I found it breeding along the valley of the Thompson and in the Okanagan, near Vernon, May and June, 1895. Mr. Brooks has found it breeding at Chilliwhack.

title: Chapter 250: Brewer's Blackbird.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Common, and generally distributed on the Mainland; quite common in the Lower Fraser Valley, Grand Prairie, south side of Thompson River, down into the Okanagan country. Breeding in suitable localities. Not common on Vancouver Island; a few have been taken near Victoria.

title: Chapter 252: Alaskan Pine Grosbeak.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Mainland east and west of Cascade Mountains. Common in winter in the neighbourhood of Clinton. Mr. Brooks reports it from Chilliwhack and Okanagan. Rare at Metlakatla. (_Rev. J. H. Keen_.)

title: Chapter 301: Bank Swallow.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: An abundant summer resident, chiefly east of Cascades, north to Cariboo District. Very common from Sicamous down through the Okanagan. Taken at Chilliwhack by A. C. Brooks.

title: Chapter 330: American Redstart.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Found through the interior of the Province, from Barkerville south to Okanagan, chiefly east of, and accidentally west of, Cascades. Taken at Chilliwhack by A. C. Brooks.

title: Chapter 338: Tule Wren.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: Abundant east of Cascades, from the lakes along the Cariboo Road, south through the Okanagan. Taken at Chilliwhack by A. C. Brooks.

title: Chapter 348: Chestnut-backed Chickadee.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: An abundant resident chiefly west of Cascades. Vancouver Island and Queen Charlotte Islands. Taken at Arrow Lake by A. C. Brooks.
Family CHAMÆIDÆ. Wren, Tits and Bush-Tits.

title: Chapter 362: Mountain Bluebird.
sourceName: Edith Wharton — The Custom of the Country (1913)
paragraphs: A summer resident east and west of Cascades, including Vancouver Island.
A. No. Albatross, Black-footed 35 Albatross, Short-tailed 36 Auklet, Rhinoceros 12 Auklet, Cassin's 13

Bittern, American 88 Blackbird, Yellow-headed 244 Blackbird, San Diego Redwing 245 Blackbird, North-western 246 Blackbird, Rusty 249 Blackbird, Brewer's 250 Bluebird, Western 361 Bluebird, Mountain 362 Bobolink 242 Brant 82 Brant, Black 83 Bunting, Lazuli 294 Bush-tit, Least 349
